name: Operational, model & conduct
description: Process, people, systems, models and behaviour — the non-financial risks.
primer:
**Operational risk** (Basel) is the risk of loss from inadequate or failed **internal processes, people and systems, or from external events**. It explicitly *includes legal risk* but *excludes strategic and reputational risk*. It is the residual category that nonetheless produces some of the largest single losses.

**Seven loss event types**: internal fraud; external fraud; employment practices; clients, products & business practices; damage to physical assets; business disruption & system failures; execution, delivery & process management. Standardising them lets internal and external data be pooled.

**Four data/assessment inputs**: *Internal loss data (ILD)* — own history, backward-looking and thin in the tail; *External loss data* — other firms' losses (consortia/public), enriches the tail but has reporting bias toward large public losses; *Scenario analysis* — expert frequency/severity for tail events absent from data; *RCSA* (risk & control self-assessment) — bottom-up identification of risks, inherent vs residual severity, and control effectiveness.

**Capital — SMA** (post-2023) replaced the standardised approaches and banks' own AMA models: $\text{ORC}=\mathrm{BIC}\times\mathrm{ILM}$. The **BIC** (Business Indicator Component) is a size proxy scaled by *progressive* coefficients (bigger bank → higher marginal rate). The **ILM** (Internal Loss Multiplier) tilts capital up/down by loss history relative to size: $\mathrm{ILM}=\ln(e^{1}-1+(\mathrm{LC}/\mathrm{BIC})^{0.8})$ — clean history pays less.

**Model risk**: loss from decisions on models that are *wrong* or *misused* (bad assumptions/data, implementation errors, out-of-domain use). Controlled by independent **model validation** (conceptual soundness, data, implementation, performance — effective challenge) and **benchmarking** (vs a challenger model or market consensus, useful where backtesting is impossible). **SR 11-7** (US guidance: inventory, ownership, tiering, validation, monitoring) is the de facto global standard.

**Conduct risk**: harm to clients/market integrity (mis-selling, market abuse, conflicts) drawing fines/redress; rooted in culture and incentives. **Reputational risk**: damage to standing that cuts revenue or raises funding cost — usually a second-order consequence, hard to capitalise directly. **Cyber risk**: attacks/failures of IT systems (breach, ransomware, DoS) threatening confidentiality, integrity, availability. **Third-party/outsourcing risk**: vendor failure disrupts the firm — *outsourcing the activity does not outsource the accountability*. **Operational resilience**: delivering critical services through disruption, measured by an **impact tolerance** — the maximum tolerable disruption to a critical service.

cards.term: Basel's definition of operational risk includes legal risk but excludes strategic and reputational risk.
cards.definition: True. Operational risk is the risk of loss from inadequate or failed internal processes, people and systems, or from external events — explicitly including legal risk, but excluding strategic and reputational risk. It is everywhere a transaction is processed, not confined to a trading book.
cards.note: Basel's formal definition; the residual category that nonetheless produces the largest single losses.
cards.summary: True — operational risk explicitly includes legal risk and excludes strategic and reputational risk.

cards.term: Basel classifies operational losses into {{seven}} standard event types, from internal fraud to execution, delivery & process management.
cards.definition: The seven categories used to classify operational losses: internal fraud; external fraud; employment practices; clients, products & business practices; damage to physical assets; business disruption & system failures; and execution, delivery & process management.
cards.note: Standardising event types lets internal and external loss data be pooled and compared.

cards.definition: A firm's own historical record of operational loss events above a collection threshold, with amounts, dates and causes. It anchors capital and risk measurement in the institution's actual experience.
cards.note: Backward-looking and sparse in the tail — rare large events are under-sampled by construction.

cards.definition: Operational losses experienced by other firms, sourced from consortia or public databases, used to enrich a firm's thin tail. It reveals plausible large events the firm hasn't suffered yet, but needs scaling for size and relevance.
cards.note: Subject to reporting bias — large, public losses are over-represented.

cards.definition: Structured expert assessment of plausible severe operational events — their frequency and severity — to capture tail risks absent from historical data. It injects forward-looking judgement where loss data is silent.
cards.note: Powerful but prone to bias; needs disciplined facilitation and challenge.

cards.definition: A bottom-up process in which business units identify their operational risks, assess inherent and residual severity, and evaluate control effectiveness. The cornerstone of qualitative operational-risk management.

cards.term: Basel's Standardised Measurement Approach (SMA) for operational-risk capital (BCBS baseline: January 2023) replaced both the older standardised approaches and banks' own {{AMA}} models.
cards.definition: Basel's single method for operational-risk capital (BCBS baseline: January 2023; EU via CRR3 from January 2025), replacing the older standardised approaches and the bank's own AMA models. It combines a size-based Business Indicator Component (BIC) with the bank's historical loss experience via the ILM.
cards.note: [1] BCBS d424 (Basel III finalisation, Dec 2017) — https://www.bis.org/bcbs/publ/d424.pdf · [2] CRR3 (EU) 2024/1623 Art. 312–315 — https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32024R1623

cards.definition: The size proxy in the SMA: a Business Indicator built from interest, services and financial statement items, scaled by progressive regulatory coefficients (the bigger the bank, the higher the marginal rate). It sets baseline operational-risk capital before loss adjustment.
cards.note: Larger banks attract higher marginal coefficients on the BI buckets.

cards.definition: An SMA scalar that tilts capital up or down according to whether a bank's historical losses are high or low relative to its size. A bank with a clean loss history pays less; a loss-heavy one pays more.

The risk of loss from decisions based on models that are wrong or misused — flawed assumptions, bad data, implementation errors, or application outside their valid domain. Pervasive wherever pricing, risk and capital depend on quantitative models.
cards.note: Two sources: the model is fundamentally wrong, or a sound model is used incorrectly.

cards.definition: Independent assessment of a model's conceptual soundness, data, implementation and ongoing performance before approval and periodically after. It is the second-line control that challenges the developers' choices.
cards.note: Effective challenge requires validators with the standing and skill to disagree.

cards.term: The de facto global standard for managing model risk is the US supervisory guidance {{SR 11-7}}.
cards.definition: The framework of inventory, ownership, tiering, validation and monitoring that controls a firm's models across their life cycle. The US SR 11-7 guidance is the de facto global standard for managing model risk.
cards.note: Treats model risk as a category to be managed like credit or market risk.

cards.definition: Comparing a model's outputs against an alternative or challenger model, or against market consensus, to detect bias or error. A validation tool especially useful where direct backtesting is impossible (e.g. long-horizon credit models).

The risk that a firm's behaviour — mis-selling, market abuse, unfair treatment of customers, conflicts of interest — harms clients or market integrity, drawing fines and redress. Culture and incentives are its root causes.
cards.note: Post-2008 conduct fines have rivalled credit losses for some banks.

cards.definition: The risk that damage to a firm's standing with customers, counterparties, investors or regulators reduces revenue or raises funding costs. Usually a second-order consequence of another risk crystallising, but capable of becoming existential.
cards.note: Hard to capitalise directly; managed through the events that trigger it.

cards.definition: The risk of loss from attacks on, or failures of, information systems — data breaches, ransomware, denial of service — threatening confidentiality, integrity and availability. A fast-growing operational risk with systemic potential through shared infrastructure.
cards.note: Increasingly addressed by dedicated regimes (e.g. EU DORA) rather than generic op-risk rules.

cards.term: Outsourcing an activity to a third-party vendor also transfers the firm's accountability for it.
cards.definition: False. Third-party / outsourcing risk is the risk that a vendor or service provider fails, is breached, or underperforms, disrupting the firm's own operations. Outsourcing the activity does not outsource the accountability, and concentration in a few critical cloud and data providers makes this a sector-wide concern.
cards.note: Outsourcing the activity does not outsource the accountability.
cards.summary: False — outsourcing the activity does not outsource the accountability.

cards.term: Operational resilience is measured by an {{impact tolerance}}: the maximum tolerable disruption to a critical business service.
cards.definition: The ability to deliver critical business services through disruption — set against defined impact tolerances — covering prevention, continuity and recovery. The supervisory focus has shifted from preventing every incident to surviving the inevitable ones.
cards.note: Measured by impact tolerance: the maximum tolerable disruption to a critical service.
